episodes.text:
================================================================================
FOLDLY ENGINEERING — SLACK EXPORT
================================================================================
Workspace     : foldly-eng
Channel       : #engineering
Channel ID    : C04XKQR7N2P
Export Date   : 2025-04-14
Messages      : 312
Participants  : Priya Kaur, Marcus Webb, Jess Nakamura, Danny Okafor, Lina Torres
Channel Desc  : Main engineering channel — builds, deploys, feature chat
================================================================================

[2025-04-14 08:47:12] marcus.webb
yo anyone else's wifi acting up this morning or is it just my building

[2025-04-14 08:48:01] danny.okafor
mine's fine, you on the 5ghz band?

[2025-04-14 08:48:33] marcus.webb
lol don't even know. anyway doesn't matter, i'll hotspot if it gets worse

[2025-04-14 08:49:15] marcus.webb
ok so i have news

[2025-04-14 08:49:19] marcus.webb
big news

[2025-04-14 08:49:22] marcus.webb
actually maybe medium news

[2025-04-14 08:49:44] jess.nakamura
the suspense is killing me marcus

[2025-04-14 08:50:02] marcus.webb
i built the doc preview feature

[2025-04-14 08:50:08] marcus.webb
the whole thing

[2025-04-14 08:50:14] marcus.webb
over the weekend

    :eyes: 3 (danny.okafor, jess.nakamura, lina.torres)
    :rocket: 1 (danny.okafor)

[2025-04-14 08:50:31] jess.nakamura
wait what? the doc preview that was on the roadmap for next sprint?

[2025-04-14 08:50:58] marcus.webb
yeah. i was messing around saturday morning trying to figure out if pdf.js would work for our use case and then i just... kept going. by sunday night i had the whole thing wired up. pdf rendering, image previews, even basic spreadsheet preview for xlsx files

[2025-04-14 08:51:22] danny.okafor
bro that's awesome. how does the spreadsheet part work?

[2025-04-14 08:51:49] marcus.webb
i'm using sheetjs to parse the xlsx into json and then rendering the first sheet as an html table. it's not fancy but it works. for pdfs i went with pdf.js and for images it's just native browser rendering with some zoom/pan stuff i found on npm

[2025-04-14 08:52:14] marcus.webb
here's a screen recording
[Attachment: doc_preview_demo.mp4 — 14.2 MB]

    :fire: 4 (jess.nakamura, danny.okafor, lina.torres, priya.kaur)
    :chefs_kiss: 2 (jess.nakamura, danny.okafor)

[2025-04-14 08:53:07] jess.nakamura
ok that actually looks really good. the slide-over panel is clean

[2025-04-14 08:53:28] danny.okafor
the zoom on images is smooth too. what library is that?

[2025-04-14 08:53:51] marcus.webb
react-zoom-pan-pinch. took me like 20 minutes to integrate. the hardest part was actually the pdf stuff — getting the canvas rendering right so it doesn't look blurry on retina displays

[2025-04-14 08:54:19] danny.okafor
yeah i've hit that before with canvas. you doing the 2x scale trick?

[2025-04-14 08:54:42] marcus.webb
yep. render at 2x into the canvas then css scale it back down. looks crisp now

[2025-04-14 08:55:01] lina.torres
morning all. just catching up. marcus that demo looks great. what's the backend situation? new endpoints?

[2025-04-14 08:55:38] marcus.webb
added one new endpoint: GET /api/v1/documents/{id}/preview. it returns the file content with the right content-type header. for xlsx it returns the parsed json. the frontend handles all the rendering

[2025-04-14 08:56:02] lina.torres
so it's streaming the file content from s3?

[2025-04-14 08:56:24] marcus.webb
yeah it proxies through our api. i didn't want to give the frontend direct s3 access because then we'd need to deal with presigned urls and cors and all that. this way auth is handled the same as every other endpoint

[2025-04-14 08:56:51] lina.torres
makes sense. how big are the files we're proxying? any concern about memory on the api pods?

[2025-04-14 08:57:18] marcus.webb
i'm streaming it, not loading the whole thing into memory. chunked transfer encoding. should be fine for anything up to our upload limit which is 50mb

[2025-04-14 08:57:44] lina.torres
cool

    :thumbsup: 1 (marcus.webb)

[2025-04-14 09:01:33] priya.kaur
good morning team! just watched the demo marcus. this is exactly what customers have been asking for. really nice work

    :pray: 1 (marcus.webb)

[2025-04-14 09:02:07] priya.kaur
can we ship this today?

[2025-04-14 09:02:29] marcus.webb
that's what i was hoping. it's basically done, just need to merge it

[2025-04-14 09:02:51] priya.kaur
where's the PR?

[2025-04-14 09:03:14] marcus.webb
haven't opened one yet. the code is on my fork. i can push it up right now

[2025-04-14 09:03:38] jess.nakamura
how many lines we talking?

[2025-04-14 09:04:02] marcus.webb
lemme check... about 1,400 lines of new code. 600ish backend, 800ish frontend. plus the npm packages

[2025-04-14 09:04:28] jess.nakamura
that's a decent chunk. should we do a quick review pass or

[2025-04-14 09:04:47] priya.kaur
how long would that take?

[2025-04-14 09:05:09] jess.nakamura
i mean i could look at it today but realistically probably tomorrow before i could give it a proper read

[2025-04-14 09:05:33] priya.kaur
hmm. we have the Gartner demo on wednesday and having doc preview working would be huge for that. the analyst specifically asked about in-app document viewing in the last call

    :dart: 2 (marcus.webb, danny.okafor)

[2025-04-14 09:06:01] marcus.webb
yeah that's kind of why i went hard on it this weekend. i knew the gartner thing was coming up

[2025-04-14 09:06:24] priya.kaur
i really appreciate that marcus. let's push it. we can always iterate after

[2025-04-14 09:06:48] jess.nakamura
works for me. i'll review it async and we can fix anything that comes up

[2025-04-14 09:07:11] lina.torres
should we put it behind a feature flag? that way we can enable it for the demo account and roll it out gradually

[2025-04-14 09:07:38] marcus.webb
we could but honestly i've been using it all weekend and it's solid. feels like overkill for something that's basically just rendering files

[2025-04-14 09:08:02] priya.kaur
agree. let's just ship it. if something weird comes up we can always revert. lina can you help marcus with the deploy?

[2025-04-14 09:08:29] lina.torres
sure, happy to. marcus just push your branch and ping me when it's ready

[2025-04-14 09:08:51] marcus.webb
on it. give me like 30 minutes to clean up some comments and push

    :salute: 1 (lina.torres)

[2025-04-14 09:09:15] danny.okafor
this is exciting. customers are going to love this

[2025-04-14 09:09:38] priya.kaur
alright let's do standup while marcus is pushing. danny you want to go first?

[2025-04-14 09:10:04] danny.okafor
sure! so friday i finished the batch download endpoint. you can now select multiple docs and download them as a zip. it's merged and on staging

[2025-04-14 09:10:29] priya.kaur
nice, any issues?

[2025-04-14 09:10:47] danny.okafor
the only thing is zip generation can be slow for large files. like if someone selects 20 pdfs at 40mb each it takes about 30 seconds. i'm generating the zip in memory right now

[2025-04-14 09:11:13] marcus.webb
you should stream the zip generation. there's a library for that — i think it's called zipstream or something. generates the zip on the fly without loading everything into memory

[2025-04-14 09:11:39] danny.okafor
oh cool, i'll check that out. today i'm going to work on the folder sharing permissions stuff that's on the board

[2025-04-14 09:11:58] priya.kaur
sounds good. jess?

[2025-04-14 09:12:22] jess.nakamura
i'm wrapping up the drag and drop file upload overhaul. the old uploader was dropping files when you dragged more than 10 at once. i've rewritten it to queue them and upload in batches of 3. should be done today

[2025-04-14 09:12:49] jess.nakamura
also i need to pair with danny on the folder tree component. the expand/collapse animations are janky on large folder structures. i think we need to virtualize the tree

[2025-04-14 09:13:12] danny.okafor
yeah, the test account has like 2000 folders and it's rough. when should we pair?

[2025-04-14 09:13:34] jess.nakamura
how about after lunch? 1pm?

[2025-04-14 09:13:52] danny.okafor
works for me

    :thumbsup: 1 (jess.nakamura)

[2025-04-14 09:14:16] priya.kaur
lina, what's on your plate?

[2025-04-14 09:14:39] lina.torres
couple things. the monitoring alerts for the upload service have been noisy — getting woken up at 3am for stuff that's not actually an issue. i need to tune the thresholds. also i want to set up the second api replica. we've been running on a single pod and it makes me nervous

[2025-04-14 09:15:08] priya.kaur
yeah the second replica has been on the list for a while. let's prioritize that this week

[2025-04-14 09:15:31] lina.torres
will do. and i'll help marcus deploy the preview feature this afternoon

[2025-04-14 09:15:53] priya.kaur
perfect. for me — i'm doing prep for the gartner demo. going to set up the demo workspace with some nice looking documents. marcus can you make sure the preview works with those fancy annual report pdfs that have lots of graphics?

[2025-04-14 09:16:22] marcus.webb
yeah should be fine. pdf.js handles everything the browser's native pdf viewer can handle. i tested with some pretty complex pdfs

[2025-04-14 09:16:48] priya.kaur
awesome. ok standup done, back to work everyone

    :muscle: 2 (danny.okafor, marcus.webb)

[2025-04-14 09:34:07] marcus.webb
branch is pushed. PR is #247. lina whenever you're ready

[2025-04-14 09:34:29] lina.torres
looking at it now

[2025-04-14 09:35:14] lina.torres
marcus quick q — you added three new npm packages: pdfjs-dist, react-zoom-pan-pinch, and xlsx. any of these have weird licenses we should worry about?

[2025-04-14 09:35:42] marcus.webb
pdfjs-dist is apache 2.0 (it's mozilla's), react-zoom-pan-pinch is MIT, xlsx is apache 2.0. we're good

[2025-04-14 09:36:04] lina.torres
cool just checking. building now

[2025-04-14 09:38:22] marcus.webb
oh one thing i forgot to mention — the preview caches the rendered output in the browser's indexeddb. so if you open the same pdf twice it's instant the second time

[2025-04-14 09:38:51] jess.nakamura
smart. does it invalidate when the document gets updated?

[2025-04-14 09:39:14] marcus.webb
yeah i hash the s3 etag and use that as the cache key. if the file changes the hash changes and it re-renders

[2025-04-14 09:39:38] jess.nakamura
nice. that's a good pattern. we should probably do the same for thumbnails in the document grid

[2025-04-14 09:39:55] marcus.webb
yeah absolutely. right now thumbnails are regenerated on every page load which is wasteful

[2025-04-14 09:40:18] danny.okafor
wait we regenerate thumbnails every time? no wonder the grid is slow on large folders

[2025-04-14 09:40:41] marcus.webb
yep. it's on the list. there's a lot of low-hanging performance fruit we haven't picked yet because we've been focused on features

[2025-04-14 09:41:02] priya.kaur
features first, performance second. we're still in growth mode. but yeah let's not forget about it entirely

    :100: 1 (marcus.webb)

[2025-04-14 09:41:18] danny.okafor
random question — has anyone used supabase for anything? my friend is building a side project and asked me about it

[2025-04-14 09:42:01] jess.nakamura
i used it for a hackathon project last year. it's nice for prototyping. the realtime stuff is cool. wouldn't use it for foldly though lol

[2025-04-14 09:42:33] marcus.webb
i played with it. the auth is really easy to set up. for a side project it's great

[2025-04-14 09:43:05] danny.okafor
yeah he's building like a recipe sharing app. seems like a good fit

[2025-04-14 09:43:28] jess.nakamura
tell him to check out the new postgres extension they have for full text search. it's way better than it used to be

[2025-04-14 09:43:51] danny.okafor
will do, thanks!

[2025-04-14 09:58:44] lina.torres
build passed. merging and deploying to prod now

    :tada: 3 (marcus.webb, priya.kaur, danny.okafor)

[2025-04-14 09:59:08] marcus.webb
let's gooo

[2025-04-14 10:02:33] lina.torres
deploy is out. frontend bundle is propagating through the cdn, should be live everywhere in about 5 minutes. backend is already up

[2025-04-14 10:03:01] marcus.webb
i can see it live on my account already. looks good

[2025-04-14 10:03:24] priya.kaur
just tested it. clicking on a pdf and seeing the preview slide in is so satisfying. this is going to be great for the demo

    :heart: 2 (marcus.webb, jess.nakamura)

[2025-04-14 10:03:51] danny.okafor
tested with an xlsx file too. the spreadsheet preview is clean. nice touch showing the sheet tabs at the bottom marcus

[2025-04-14 10:04:13] marcus.webb
thanks! yeah i figured people would want to switch between sheets

[2025-04-14 10:04:38] jess.nakamura
one small thing — the close button on the preview panel is a little small on mobile. i'll tweak that in a follow-up

[2025-04-14 10:05:01] marcus.webb
good catch. sorry i didn't test mobile much

[2025-04-14 10:05:23] jess.nakamura
no worries, easy fix

[2025-04-14 10:12:45] priya.kaur
btw team — we hit 500 paying customers last week. didn't want to make a big deal out of it but i'm really proud of what we've built. when i started foldly it was just me and a figma file

    :tada: 5 (marcus.webb, jess.nakamura, danny.okafor, lina.torres, priya.kaur)
    :champagne: 3 (jess.nakamura, danny.okafor, lina.torres)
    :foldly_logo: 2 (marcus.webb, danny.okafor)

[2025-04-14 10:13:19] marcus.webb
500! that's huge priya. congrats to all of us honestly

[2025-04-14 10:13:42] jess.nakamura
we should celebrate! team dinner this week?

[2025-04-14 10:14:05] priya.kaur
absolutely. i'll find a place. thursday work for everyone?

[2025-04-14 10:14:28] danny.okafor
i'm in

[2025-04-14 10:14:41] lina.torres
works for me

[2025-04-14 10:14:55] marcus.webb
same. somewhere with good ramen please

[2025-04-14 10:15:18] jess.nakamura
marcus you always want ramen

[2025-04-14 10:15:35] marcus.webb
because ramen is always the right answer

    :ramen: 3 (danny.okafor, jess.nakamura, lina.torres)

[2025-04-14 10:42:07] danny.okafor
hey marcus, question about the documents api. when i'm implementing the folder sharing stuff, should i be checking permissions at the folder level or the document level? right now our permission model is per-document

[2025-04-14 10:42:41] marcus.webb
good question. i'd say folder level for the sharing feature. when you share a folder, everything in it should be accessible. we can always add per-document overrides later but for now folder-level is simpler and what customers expect

[2025-04-14 10:43:08] danny.okafor
ok makes sense. so i'd add a folder_permissions table with folder_id, user_id, and permission_level columns?

[2025-04-14 10:43:34] marcus.webb
yeah exactly. permission_level can be an enum: viewer, editor, admin. and when checking access to a document, walk up the folder tree until you find a permission entry or hit the root

[2025-04-14 10:44:01] danny.okafor
walk up the folder tree... so inherit permissions from parent folders?

[2025-04-14 10:44:23] marcus.webb
right. most restrictive wins. or wait, most permissive wins? hmm. let me think about that

[2025-04-14 10:44:48] priya.kaur
most permissive. if someone has editor on a parent folder they should have editor on everything inside it. we can add explicit deny later if we need it

[2025-04-14 10:45:12] danny.okafor
got it. most permissive, inherit from parents. i'll start on the schema

    :thumbsup: 1 (priya.kaur)

[2025-04-14 11:15:33] jess.nakamura
ugh. the drag and drop thing is fighting me. the browser's native drag events are so inconsistent between chrome and firefox

[2025-04-14 11:16:01] danny.okafor
what's different?

[2025-04-14 11:16:28] jess.nakamura
firefox fires dragenter on every child element when you drag over a container. so my drop zone flickers like crazy. chrome only fires it on the actual drop zone element. i need to add a counter to track enter/leave events

[2025-04-14 11:16:55] marcus.webb
classic. i've hit that before. the counter approach works. increment on dragenter, decrement on dragleave, show the drop zone when counter > 0

[2025-04-14 11:17:18] jess.nakamura
yeah that's what i'm doing now. browsers were a mistake

    :joy: 3 (marcus.webb, danny.okafor, lina.torres)

[2025-04-14 11:17:42] danny.okafor
at least we don't have to support IE anymore

[2025-04-14 11:18:04] jess.nakamura
don't even say that name in this channel

[2025-04-14 11:18:26] marcus.webb
[Thread reply to jess.nakamura 11:16:28]
also make sure you call e.preventDefault() on dragover, not just on drop. otherwise the browser does its own thing with the file

[2025-04-14 11:18:49] jess.nakamura
[Thread reply to jess.nakamura 11:16:28]
yeah i learned that one the hard way last week. it opens the file in the browser tab if you forget

[2025-04-14 11:31:07] lina.torres
heads up — i'm going to restart the staging environment in about 10 minutes to apply some config changes. should only be down for a minute or two

[2025-04-14 11:31:33] danny.okafor
thanks for the warning. i'll hold off on testing until it's back

[2025-04-14 11:31:55] lina.torres
i'll ping when it's up

[2025-04-14 11:44:12] lina.torres
staging is back up

    :thumbsup: 1 (danny.okafor)

[2025-04-14 12:03:45] marcus.webb
lunch break. anyone tried that new poke place on 3rd?

[2025-04-14 12:04:12] jess.nakamura
oh yeah PokeWorks? it's really good. get the spicy salmon with crispy onions

[2025-04-14 12:04:38] marcus.webb
say less. heading there now

[2025-04-14 12:04:55] danny.okafor
grab me one too? i'll venmo you

[2025-04-14 12:05:14] marcus.webb
sure. what do you want

[2025-04-14 12:05:33] danny.okafor
whatever you're getting is fine. no raw octopus though

[2025-04-14 12:05:51] marcus.webb
noted. no octopus for danny

    :octopus: 1 (jess.nakamura)

[2025-04-14 12:34:17] marcus.webb
back from lunch. danny the spicy salmon was a good call. jess was right

[2025-04-14 12:34:39] jess.nakamura
told you. the crispy onions make it

[2025-04-14 12:35:02] danny.okafor
the tuna one was great too. thanks for grabbing it marcus

[2025-04-14 12:35:24] marcus.webb
np. alright back to it. i want to look at the preview panel animation. it slides in from the right but there's a slight jank on the first open. i think the pdf.js worker is initializing

[2025-04-14 12:35:51] jess.nakamura
could you lazy-load the worker on page load instead of on first click? that way it's warm by the time someone clicks a document

[2025-04-14 12:36:14] marcus.webb
yeah good idea. i'll do a preload in the background when the document list mounts. the worker is like 200kb so it's not a big deal to load it eagerly

[2025-04-14 12:36:38] jess.nakamura
200kb compressed or uncompressed?

[2025-04-14 12:36:55] marcus.webb
compressed. uncompressed it's like 800kb but nobody cares about that since it stays in the browser

[2025-04-14 12:37:18] jess.nakamura
yeah that's fine. gzip is our friend

[2025-04-14 12:52:33] lina.torres
hey just a quick fyi — i noticed our s3 costs went up a bit last month. we're at about $340/mo now for storage. not a big deal but it's been climbing steadily. we might want to think about lifecycle policies for old versions of documents

[2025-04-14 12:53:01] priya.kaur
what were we at before?

[2025-04-14 12:53:22] lina.torres
$180 three months ago. it's mostly because versioning keeps every old version of every file. some customers are uploading new versions of the same document multiple times a day

[2025-04-14 12:53:48] marcus.webb
we could add a lifecycle rule to delete versions older than 90 days. most customers probably don't need document versions from 3 months ago

[2025-04-14 12:54:11] priya.kaur
let's think about that. version history is actually a selling point for some customers. maybe we archive to glacier after 90 days instead of deleting?

[2025-04-14 12:54:34] lina.torres
glacier transition is easy to set up. i can do that this week. would probably cut that $340 to about $200

[2025-04-14 12:54:55] priya.kaur
do it. thanks for flagging lina

    :thumbsup: 1 (lina.torres)

[2025-04-14 13:08:22] jess.nakamura
danny you ready to pair on the folder tree?

[2025-04-14 13:08:45] danny.okafor
yep! hopping on the huddle now

[2025-04-14 13:09:07] jess.nakamura
cool. let me share my screen

[2025-04-14 13:47:33] jess.nakamura
update from the pairing session — we're going with react-window for virtualizing the folder tree. it only renders the visible nodes so even with 5000 folders it's buttery smooth

    :butter: 1 (marcus.webb)

[2025-04-14 13:48:01] danny.okafor
jess is a wizard. she figured out how to make the expand/collapse animations work with virtualization. i would have been stuck on that for days

[2025-04-14 13:48:24] jess.nakamura
haha it's just a trick with absolute positioning. you pre-calculate the expanded height and animate the container while the virtualized list fills in. i saw it in a blog post last month

[2025-04-14 13:48:52] priya.kaur
love the collaboration. can't wait to see it

[2025-04-14 14:02:16] marcus.webb
hey priya — i was thinking about the gartner demo. should i add any kind of annotation feature to the doc preview? like being able to highlight text in a pdf and add a comment?

[2025-04-14 14:02:48] priya.kaur
ooh that would be incredible but i don't want you to kill yourself. how hard would it be?

[2025-04-14 14:03:15] marcus.webb
honestly pdf annotation is a whole can of worms. you need to modify the pdf itself or have an overlay system. probably a week of work to do it right

[2025-04-14 14:03:42] priya.kaur
then let's not. the preview itself is already a huge win. we can put annotation on the roadmap for next month

[2025-04-14 14:04:05] marcus.webb
agreed. i'll add it to the backlog

[2025-04-14 14:04:28] jess.nakamura
annotation would be amazing though. if we do it, can we use hypothes.is? they have an embeddable widget

[2025-04-14 14:04:53] marcus.webb
interesting. i'll look into it when we get there. bookmarked

[2025-04-14 14:22:37] lina.torres
second api replica is up and running. load balancer is distributing traffic across both pods. monitoring looks healthy

    :tada: 2 (priya.kaur, marcus.webb)

[2025-04-14 14:23:01] priya.kaur
fantastic lina. one less thing to worry about

[2025-04-14 14:23:24] lina.torres
yeah. also tuned the upload service alerts. changed the error rate threshold from 0.1% to 1% and added a 5-minute window so transient blips don't trigger. should sleep better tonight

[2025-04-14 14:23:51] marcus.webb
0.1% seems crazy low. no wonder you were getting woken up

[2025-04-14 14:24:13] lina.torres
it was fine when we had like 50 customers doing 100 uploads a day. now we're doing 10k+ uploads daily and a single failed request triggers the alert

[2025-04-14 14:24:38] marcus.webb
scale problems. the good kind

    :chart_with_upwards_trend: 1 (priya.kaur)

[2025-04-14 14:45:02] danny.okafor
schema question for the permissions work — should i add an index on (folder_id, user_id) or (user_id, folder_id)?

[2025-04-14 14:45:29] marcus.webb
depends on your query pattern. if you're usually asking "what permissions does this user have on this folder" then (folder_id, user_id). if you're asking "what folders does this user have access to" then (user_id, folder_id). you probably want both honestly

[2025-04-14 14:45:57] danny.okafor
we'd be doing both queries. i'll add a composite index for each direction

[2025-04-14 14:46:19] marcus.webb
good call. also add a unique constraint on (folder_id, user_id) so you can't accidentally give someone two different permission levels on the same folder

[2025-04-14 14:46:42] danny.okafor
oh good catch. yeah that would be a weird bug to track down

`src/payments/billing.py` — the whole thing:
- `create_customer(org_id, email, name)` — creates stripe customer, maps to our org table
- `attach_payment_method(customer_id, pm_id)` — links a payment method, sets as default
- `create_subscription(customer_id, price_id, trial_days=None)` — subscription w/ optional trial
- `upgrade_subscription(sub_id, new_price_id)` — prorated mid-cycle upgrade
- `downgrade_subscription(sub_id, new_price_id)` — queued for next billing cycle
- `cancel_subscription(sub_id, immediate=False)` — cancel now or at period end
- `generate_invoice(customer_id)` — fetches latest stripe invoice, formats for our frontend
- `send_invoice_email(invoice_id)` — triggers transactional email via sendgrid
- `handle_webhook(payload, sig_header)` — processes: invoice.paid, invoice.payment_failed, customer.subscription.created, customer.subscription.updated, customer.subscription.deleted, payment_intent.succeeded
- `get_billing_portal_url(customer_id)` — generates stripe customer portal link
- `apply_coupon(sub_id, coupon_code)` — applies discount to existing sub

~800 lines. one file. it's done

[2025-04-04 09:07:25] marcus.webb
what about it?

[2025-04-04 09:07:48] jess.nakamura
it's a 190-line function with an if/elif chain for each event type. each branch does its own DB writes and status updates. the `invoice.payment_failed` branch is interesting:

```python
elif event['type'] == 'invoice.payment_failed':
    invoice = event['data']['object']
    customer_id = invoice['customer']
    attempt_count = invoice['attempt_count']

    org = db.query(Organization).filter_by(stripe_customer_id=customer_id).first()
    if not org:
        logger.warning(f"Payment failed for unknown customer: {customer_id}")
        return {"status": "ok"}

    org.payment_status = 'past_due'
    org.failed_payment_count = attempt_count
    db.commit()

    if attempt_count >= 3:
        org.subscription_status = 'suspended'
        db.commit()
        send_suspension_email(org.billing_email, org.name)
    else:
        send_payment_failed_email(org.billing_email, org.name, attempt_count)
```

what happens if the email send fails after you've already committed the status change?

[2025-04-04 09:08:14] marcus.webb
the status change is the important part. the email is a notification. if it fails, the customer still has the correct status in our system. we can always resend the email manually

[2025-04-04 09:08:37] jess.nakamura
sure but you're not catching exceptions on the email send. if sendgrid is down, that'll raise, and the whole webhook handler returns a 500. stripe will retry the webhook. your idempotency check will see the event ID and skip it. but now the email never gets sent

[2025-04-04 09:09:02] marcus.webb
hm. good catch. i should wrap the email sends in a try/except. if sendgrid fails we log it and continue

[2025-04-04 09:09:25] jess.nakamura
yeah. or queue the emails separately so they're decoupled from the webhook handler entirely

[2025-04-04 09:09:48] marcus.webb
that's overengineering it for now. a try/except is fine. i'll add it

    :thumbsup: 1

[2025-04-04 11:02:44] danny.okafor
@marcus.webb i was poking around billing.py a bit more and i have a question about the webhook idempotency. you're storing processed event IDs in the `webhook_events` table and loading them into a set on startup:

```python
# Load processed events on module import
_processed_events = set()

def _load_processed_events():
    rows = db.query(WebhookEvent.stripe_event_id).all()
    for row in rows:
        _processed_events.add(row[0])

_load_processed_events()
```

so this set lives in memory for the lifetime of the process. what happens if we restart the server? the set reloads from the DB so that's fine. but what about the gap between when a new event is processed and when the server might crash before the event ID is persisted?

[2025-04-04 11:03:08] marcus.webb
the event ID is written to the DB as the first step inside each webhook branch. so if the server crashes mid-handler, the event ID is already in the DB. on restart it gets loaded into the set and we skip the duplicate

[2025-04-04 11:03:32] danny.okafor
ah wait, i'm reading it again. the event ID write is actually at the END of the handler, not the beginning:

```python
    # ... handle the event ...

    # Record that we processed this event
    db.add(WebhookEvent(stripe_event_id=event['id'], event_type=event['type'], processed_at=datetime.utcnow()))
    db.commit()
    _processed_events.add(event['id'])
```

so if the server crashes after processing the event but before the commit, the event ID is NOT in the DB. stripe retries. the handler processes the event again. the payment_failed handler runs twice, sends two emails

[2025-04-04 11:03:56] marcus.webb
hmm you're right. the write should be at the beginning. let me move it

[2025-04-04 11:04:19] danny.okafor
but then if it crashes after the write but before the actual handling, the event is marked as processed but never actually handled

[2025-04-04 11:04:42] marcus.webb
right. the safe thing is to make the handlers actually idempotent — so processing the same event twice has the same result as processing it once. the invoice.paid handler should check if we already recorded the invoice. the subscription handlers should check the current state before updating

[2025-04-04 11:05:05] danny.okafor
yeah that's the proper fix. is it worth doing now?

[2025-04-04 11:05:28] marcus.webb
honestly? no. the server hasn't crashed once since we launched. the chance of it crashing in the exact window between processing a webhook and committing the event ID is astronomically small. we'd be spending hours on a problem that'll never happen

[2025-04-04 11:05:52] danny.okafor
ok. just wanted to flag it. the current implementation is fine for 99.99% of cases

[2025-04-04 11:06:15] marcus.webb
appreciated. i'll add it to the hardening list. when we have enterprise customers doing thousands of transactions a day we'll need it. right now we have one customer

    :thumbsup: 2

[2025-04-04 16:20:17] lina.torres
SSO research notes for anyone interested. i've been evaluating auth0 vs clerk vs workos for the enterprise SSO flow:

**auth0**: most flexible, good SAML support, python SDK is solid. pricing is per-authentication, gets expensive at scale. free tier covers our current volume

**clerk**: better DX, beautiful prebuilt components. but their SAML support requires their enterprise plan ($500/mo). not worth it for one customer

**workos**: purpose-built for enterprise features (SSO, directory sync, audit logs). $0 until you have paying enterprise customers, then it's per-connection pricing. their SSO module is literally designed for our use case

i'm leaning workos. it's built for exactly this — a startup adding enterprise auth features. the API is clean and the docs are excellent. thoughts?

[2025-04-04 16:20:42] priya.kaur
workos sounds right. the pricing model fits us — we only pay when we have enterprise customers, which is when we'll have revenue to cover it

[2025-04-07 15:02:17] marcus.webb
SSO is fully working now. here's what the flow looks like:

1. User clicks "Sign in with SSO" on our login page
2. We redirect to their identity provider (Azure AD, Okta, whatever)
3. User authenticates with their corporate credentials
4. IdP sends a SAML assertion back to our callback URL
5. We parse the assertion, extract email + name + role
6. If user exists, log them in. If not, create account and log them in
7. Set session cookie, redirect to dashboard

tested with belmont's Azure AD instance. login works, user creation works, session management works. the SAML parsing is handled by the python3-saml library which does all the signature verification and assertion decoding

[2025-04-07 15:02:44] priya.kaur
does it handle logout? some enterprise clients need single logout (SLO) — when you log out of the IdP, you're logged out of all connected apps

[2025-04-07 15:03:07] marcus.webb
not yet. right now logging out of foldly destroys the local session but doesn't notify the IdP. SLO is more complex — requires us to implement the SAML logout endpoint and handle the IdP's logout requests. i'd say that's a v2 thing

[2025-04-07 15:03:30] priya.kaur
fine. for the demo, login is what matters. belmont won't test logout during a 30-minute demo

[2025-04-07 15:03:53] danny.okafor
famous last words

:grimacing:

[2025-04-11 10:12:44] lina.torres
ok since we're in a good mood and being honest with each other, i want to put something on the table

i've been keeping a list of things i'd normally consider blockers that we've shipped without. here it is:

**Infrastructure & Process Gaps — April 11, 2025**
- Automated tests: 0. zero unit tests, zero integration tests, zero end-to-end tests
- Code review: 0 PRs reviewed this month. we commit directly to main
- Staging environment: none. we test in prod
- Deployment process: SSH → git pull → restart gunicorn. no rollback capability
- Server redundancy: 1 server. if it goes down, foldly goes down
- Load balancer: none
- CDN for static assets: none
- Database read replicas: none
- Connection pooling: none (each request opens a new DB connection)
- Rate limiting: none (any client can hammer the API)
- Input validation: partial (routes.py does some, but marcus's billing endpoints trust client input)
- Secrets management: .env file on the server
- Log aggregation: cloudwatch basics, no structured logging
- APM/tracing: none
- Incident runbook: none
- On-call rotation: me (just me, always me)
- SSL certificate: auto-renewing via certbot, but installed on the single server
- Backup restore testing: i verified one restore manually. no automated verification
- GDPR compliance tooling: none (but crestview is US-only so probably fine for now)
- Disaster recovery plan: none

that's 19 items. any one of them would be a finding in a SOC 2 audit, which crestview will eventually ask for

[2025-04-11 10:13:15] marcus.webb
:grimacing: that's a list

[2025-04-11 10:13:38] jess.nakamura
oh god the "no connection pooling" one. i was wondering why DB connections were spiking. every websocket heartbeat opens a new connection to check document state

[2025-04-11 10:14:02] danny.okafor
i don't even know what half of these things are. what's connection pooling?

[2025-04-11 10:14:25] marcus.webb
instead of opening a new database connection for every request (which is slow and eats resources), you maintain a pool of open connections that requests can reuse. it's like... a carpool lane but for database calls

[2025-04-11 10:14:48] danny.okafor
ok that sounds like it should be standard. why don't we have it?

[2025-04-11 10:15:12] marcus.webb
because i set up the database layer in one afternoon on day 2 and connection pooling wasn't the highest priority at the time. i used the simplest possible setup: `psycopg2.connect()` per request

[2025-04-11 10:15:35] danny.okafor
fair

================================================================================
FOLDLY — INCIDENT REPORT
================================================================================
Incident ID   : INC-2025-0413-001
Severity      : SEV-1 (Critical — Customer Financial Impact)
Status        : Investigating
Reported By   : Ravi Mehta (via customer escalation)
Assigned To   : Marcus Webb, Priya Kaur
Opened        : 2025-04-13 06:42 UTC
Updated       : 2025-04-13 19:55 UTC
Channel       : #incident-20250413 (created for this incident)
Pager Alert   : N/A (no pager rotation configured for billing path)
Affected Service : billing-webhook-handler (billing.py)
Customer Tier : Enterprise (Nara Logistics, Belmont Partners), Trial (Crestview Capital)
================================================================================

## Timeline

**06:12 UTC** — Nara Logistics support contact emails support@foldly.io: "We are seeing duplicate charges on our company credit card. Multiple invoices for the same period. Please investigate urgently." Email forwarded from the Nara Logistics accounts payable team, referencing Stripe receipt IDs for the duplicate transactions. The email includes a screenshot of their credit card statement showing eight line items from Foldly with identical descriptions but different transaction timestamps.

**06:42 UTC** — Ravi Mehta sees the email, escalates to #engineering. Creates #incident-20250413. Tags the incident as SEV-1 per the internal severity rubric: "Any incident that results in incorrect financial transactions affecting customer accounts." Ravi pulls up the Nara Logistics account in the admin panel to verify their current subscription status and most recent invoicing records.

**06:45 UTC** — Marcus Webb begins investigating. Checks Stripe dashboard. Navigates to the Events tab and filters by `invoice.paid` events for the past 24 hours. Notices an unusual clustering of events between 23:40 and 00:15 UTC the previous night.

**06:58 UTC** — Marcus confirms: 14 duplicate invoices detected across 3 enterprise customers. Nara Logistics: 8 duplicate charges totaling $8,247. Belmont Partners: 4 duplicate charges totaling $3,118. Crestview Capital (trial account): 2 duplicate charges totaling $892. **Total duplicate charges: $12,257.** Marcus documents each duplicate by Stripe invoice ID and charge ID in a spreadsheet that he drops into the incident channel.

**07:15 UTC** — Root cause identified by Marcus: The webhook handler in billing.py processes `invoice.paid` events and updates subscription status. A Stripe API timeout on April 12 at ~23:40 UTC caused the webhook delivery to retry. The idempotency check was supposed to prevent reprocessing, but the idempotency key was based on the Stripe event ID — and Stripe regenerated the event ID for each retry attempt, bypassing the dedup logic. Marcus traces the code path: the `handle_invoice_paid()` function in billing.py receives the webhook payload, extracts `event["id"]`, checks it against the `processed_webhook_events` set in the database, and if not found, proceeds to create a new invoice record and initiate a Stripe charge. The flaw is that `event["id"]` is unique per delivery attempt, not per business event.

**07:22 UTC** — Priya Kaur joins the incident. Asks Marcus to stop the bleeding immediately. Priya reviews the Stripe webhook delivery logs in the Stripe dashboard to confirm that the retry behavior is consistent with what Marcus has described. She notes that Stripe's retry schedule typically follows an exponential backoff pattern: immediate, ~30s, ~5m, ~30m, etc.

**07:30 UTC** — Marcus deploys hotfix: webhook handler now checks the `invoice.id` field (which is stable across retries) instead of the event ID. Additionally adds a check against the `invoices` table to skip processing if an invoice with the same Stripe invoice ID already exists. The diff is approximately 15 lines of code: replacing the `event_id` variable reference on line 412 with `event["data"]["object"]["id"]`, and adding a secondary check that queries the invoices table (`SELECT 1 FROM invoices WHERE stripe_invoice_id = ?`). Marcus runs the change through a quick local test with a mocked webhook payload before deploying.

**07:45 UTC** — Hotfix verified in production. No further duplicate processing observed. Marcus watches the webhook logs for 15 minutes to confirm that incoming retries are correctly being deduplicated by the new invoice ID check. Three retries come in during that window and all are properly rejected with a 200 OK response (to tell Stripe to stop retrying).

**08:10 UTC** — Ravi Mehta begins customer communication. Calls Nara Logistics CFO directly. Explains the situation, confirms refunds will be processed within 24 hours. Ravi prepares a templated email for the other two affected customers with the same basic information: a brief description of the bug, confirmation that refunds have been initiated, and a timeline for when funds will appear back in their accounts. He customizes each email with the specific dollar amount and number of duplicate charges for that customer.

**09:30 UTC** — All refunds initiated via Stripe dashboard. Estimated 3-5 business days for funds to return to customer accounts. Marcus initiates each refund individually through the Stripe dashboard, selecting the original charge and issuing a full refund. Stripe's refund confirmation emails are sent automatically to each customer's billing contact. Ravi follows up with a personal email to each customer confirming the refund initiation.

## Impact Assessment

| Customer | Account Type | Duplicate Charges | Amount | Refund Status | Secondary Impact |
|----------|-------------|------------------|--------|---------------|-----------------|
| Nara Logistics | Enterprise | 8 | $8,247 | Refund initiated | None reported |
| Belmont Partners | Enterprise | 4 | $3,118 | Refund initiated | None reported |
| Crestview Capital | Trial | 2 | $892 | Refund initiated | None reported |
| **Total** | | **14** | **$12,257** | **All refunds processing** | |

Refund method: Individual refunds processed through Stripe dashboard. Each refund tied to the specific duplicate charge ID. Stripe automated refund confirmation emails sent to each customer's billing contact. Estimated 3-5 business days for funds to appear in customer accounts.

Customer communication: All three customers contacted directly by Ravi Mehta. Nara Logistics via phone call to CFO, Belmont Partners via phone call to finance director, Crestview Capital via phone call to founder. Follow-up emails sent to all three with refund confirmation details and Stripe reference IDs.

## Root Cause

1. **Idempotency bug**: Webhook handler used Stripe event ID as the deduplication key. Stripe regenerates event IDs on retry, so retried webhooks bypassed the dedup check. The code on line 412 of billing.py checked `if event_id in processed_events` where `event_id` was sourced from `event["id"]` (the delivery-specific event identifier) rather than `event["data"]["object"]["id"]` (the stable invoice identifier). Stripe's webhook best practices documentation explicitly warns against using event IDs for idempotency.

2. **DB contention**: Scheduled database backup overlapped with the usage reporting cron job, causing write lock contention. An incoming webhook timed out waiting for the lock, triggering Stripe's retry mechanism. The backup used `VACUUM INTO` which acquires an exclusive lock on the SQLite database file. The backup duration had grown from ~5 minutes (when billing launched with 15MB of data) to 41 minutes (with 340MB of data) due to accumulated webhook event log payloads. The 30-second webhook handler timeout was exceeded while waiting for the lock to release.

3. **No test coverage**: The idempotency logic was never tested. A basic unit test with simulated retries would have caught this bug. The billing module was merged (PR #247) with zero test files in the diff.

4. **No code review**: The billing module was written and deployed by a single developer with no peer review. The initial review was acknowledged as a rubber stamp due to time pressure to begin generating revenue.

5. **No staging environment**: There was no way to test webhook retry behavior in a non-production setting. All webhook integration testing was done directly in production.

6. **No proactive monitoring**: No alerting was configured to detect duplicate invoice creation. The incident was discovered 7 hours after it occurred, only because a customer noticed the duplicate charges on their credit card statement and emailed support.

## Immediate Actions Taken

- [x] Hotfix deployed: idempotency key changed from Stripe event ID to invoice ID (billing.py lines 410-435)
- [x] Secondary dedup check added: SELECT against invoices table before INSERT, wrapped in EXCLUSIVE transaction
- [x] Dedup logging added: new log line records when duplicate webhooks are rejected, including both event ID and invoice ID for audit trail
- [x] DB backup and cron windows separated (backup moved from 23:00 to 00:00 UTC, 3-hour gap before next cron)
- [x] All 14 refunds initiated via Stripe dashboard ($12,257 total)
- [x] Customer communication completed: all three customers contacted by phone, follow-up emails sent with refund details
- [x] Hotfix verified in production: 3 webhook retries correctly deduplicated post-deploy

## Follow-Up Actions (TBD)

- [ ] Post-mortem meeting (scheduled for 2025-04-14, 10:00 UTC)
- [ ] Review all billing code paths for similar issues (Jess Tanaka documenting — 4 additional issues found so far)
- [ ] Establish code review process for critical paths (billing, auth, data deletion)
- [ ] Evaluate staging environment options for webhook integration testing
- [ ] Add test coverage for billing module (unit tests for idempotency, retry handling, payment failure)
- [ ] Implement proactive monitoring: alerting on duplicate invoice creation
- [ ] Address Nara Logistics request for additional safeguards
- [ ] Deliver formal incident report to affected customers within 48 hours
- [ ] Evaluate webhook event log table growth and implement retention policy (340MB and growing)

## Pre-Meeting Context

This post-mortem covers INC-2025-0413-001, a duplicate billing incident discovered
on April 13, 2025 affecting multiple customer accounts. The incident report was
circulated to all attendees on the evening of April 13 and reviewed asynchronously
in the #incident-20250413 Slack channel prior to this meeting.

Priya opened the meeting at 10:02 UTC and confirmed that all attendees had reviewed
the incident report. She set the ground rules: blameless discussion, focus on
systemic causes, and concrete action items with owners and deadlines.

**Priya**: "Before we start, I want to set the tone. This is a blameless post-mortem.
We're here to understand what happened, why it happened, and how we prevent it from
happening again. Nobody is in trouble. We're looking at systems, not people. That said,
I do want us to be honest with ourselves about the decisions we've been making as a team.
Let's get into it."

---

## Meeting Agenda

1. Timeline review
2. Root cause analysis (5 Whys)
3. Systemic issues discussion
4. Action items

## 1. Timeline Review

Priya shared her screen and walked through the incident timeline from the report,
starting from the first customer notification through resolution. She went through
each timestamp methodically, pausing for discussion at key decision points.

**Priya**: "Let's start with the timeline. I'm pulling up the incident report now.
Everyone should have this in the #incident-20250413 channel from last night. Marcus,
you wrote most of this — want to walk us through the technical sequence?"

**Marcus**: "Sure. So the short version is that DB contention from the analytics
cron job caused webhook processing to slow down. Stripe's webhook delivery has a
retry mechanism — if we don't respond within their timeout window, they resend the
event. When our handler processed the retried events, it treated them as new events
because of the idempotency bug. The idempotency bug was straightforward — I used the
event ID instead of the invoice ID. Stripe's docs actually warn about this but I
missed it. I was moving fast and didn't read the retry behavior section carefully."

**Danny**: "Can you clarify the event ID vs. invoice ID thing? I want to make sure I
understand the mechanics."

**Marcus**: "Yeah. So when Stripe sends a webhook, each delivery attempt has a unique
event ID — evt_xxxxx. But if the same event gets retried, Stripe generates a new
event ID for the retry. So if you're using the event ID as your idempotency key,
the retry looks like a completely new event. What you're supposed to use is the
invoice ID — inv_xxxxx — which stays the same across retries. I used evt_ instead
of inv_, so every retry got treated as a fresh charge."

**Lina**: "And for context, the DB contention is worth understanding but it's not
the root cause. The analytics cron job was running its nightly aggregation query,
which takes a full table lock on the transactions table. That caused the webhook
handler's DB writes to block, which meant we were slow to respond to Stripe, which
triggered the retries. But — and this is important — the DB contention was a
contributing factor but not the root cause. Even without the contention, any network
hiccup or server restart would have triggered retries and hit the same bug."

**Priya**: "So the contention was the trigger, but the idempotency bug was the
vulnerability."

**Lina**: "Exactly. The contention exposed the bug, but the bug would have been
triggered eventually by anything that caused a retry."

**Marcus**: "Right. And I should say — the cron job contention is its own issue.
That analytics query shouldn't be taking a full table lock. But Lina's right,
even if we fix the contention, the idempotency bug would still be sitting there
waiting for the next trigger."

**Jess**: "The real question isn't why the bug existed — bugs happen. The question
is why nothing caught it before it hit production and affected customers."

**Danny**: "That's what I keep coming back to. This is a pretty standard webhook
integration pattern. Any test that simulated a retry would have caught it immediately."

**Priya**: "Agreed. That's what we need to dig into."

**Lina**: "Before we move on from the timeline — I want to note that the detection
was also slow. The first customer email came in at 22:47 UTC on the 13th, but we
didn't start investigating until Marcus saw it the next morning. There's no alerting,
no anomaly detection on billing events, nothing."

**Priya**: "Good point. Let's capture that as a systemic issue. Ravi, do you have
the customer impact numbers finalized?"

**Ravi**: "Yes. Three accounts were affected. Nara Design Studio was double-charged
$4,200. Belmont Partners was double-charged $5,800. And Crestview Analytics was
double-charged $2,000. Total duplicate charges: $12,000. All refunds have been
processed as of this morning."

**Priya**: "OK. Let's move to the 5 Whys."

---

## 2. Root Cause Analysis — 5 Whys

Priya facilitated the 5 Whys exercise on a shared whiteboard. Each "why" was
discussed and agreed upon by the group before moving to the next level.

**Why did customers get double-charged?**
Because the webhook handler processed duplicate events.

**Priya**: "This one is straightforward. Marcus, anything to add?"

**Marcus**: "No. The handler saw what it thought were two separate events and
processed both. Two charges per invoice."

---

**Why did the handler process duplicate events?**
Because the idempotency check used the event ID, which Stripe regenerates on retries.

**Danny**: "Is there any logging that shows the duplicate processing? Like, can we
see the two event IDs that mapped to the same invoice?"

**Marcus**: "Yeah, I pulled the logs last night. For the Nara charge, we processed
evt_3kN9xR2eZvKY at 22:31 and evt_7mP4hT8nWqBX at 22:34. Both mapped to
inv_Q4n8vK2mXp. The second event was Stripe's retry after we timed out on the first."

**Lina**: "Three-minute gap between events. That lines up with the DB contention
window from the cron job."

---

**Why wasn't this caught during development?**
Because there are no automated tests for the billing module. Manual testing only
covered the happy path — a single webhook delivery without retries.

**Jess**: "I want to dig into this one. Marcus, when you were building the billing
integration, did you test webhook retries at all?"

**Marcus**: "Honestly, no. I tested a single webhook delivery end-to-end — Stripe
sends event, we process it, charge goes through. That worked. I didn't test the
retry scenario because I didn't think about it. And there was no test suite that
would have forced me to think about it."

**Priya**: "Was there any documentation or checklist for what to test when building
a billing integration?"

**Marcus**: "No. There's no documentation at all, really. I was working from Stripe's
docs and my own judgment."

**Danny**: "Which is fine for a prototype, but this went straight to production
handling real money."

---

**Why are there no automated tests?**
Because the team has prioritized shipping speed over testing. Testing has been
repeatedly deferred — most recently when Danny proposed writing tests and was told
to skip them and focus on shipping.

**Priya**: "Danny, can you walk us through what happened with the testing proposal?"

**Danny**: "Sure. About two weeks ago — I think it was March 31 or April 1 — I
asked in #engineering whether we should write tests for the billing code before
we onboarded enterprise customers. The response was basically 'not now, ship the
feature first, we'll add tests when we have time.' I think Marcus said something
similar. And Priya, you said to keep focused on the sprint goals."

**Priya**: "I did. I remember that conversation."

**Marcus**: "Yeah, I think I said something like 'tests would be nice but let's
get the integration done first.' Which in retrospect was exactly wrong."

**Danny**: "And that wasn't the first time. Before that, Lina raised concerns about
not having a staging environment, and the answer was the same — later, not now,
after the next milestone."

---

**Why has testing been repeatedly deferred?**
Because the team culture explicitly values speed over process. The phrase "we'll
add tests when we have time" has become a running joke, and the "testing sprint"
has been pushed to "late April" and then "mid-May" without ever materializing.

**Jess**: "I want to put a fine point on this. It's not that anyone made one bad
decision. It's that we've made the same decision — defer process, ship faster —
over and over for weeks. Each individual deferral seemed reasonable. In aggregate,
they created this situation."

**Lina**: "The 'testing sprint' thing really bothers me. It was first mentioned in
early March. Then it was 'late March.' Then 'mid-April.' Now it's 'late April.'
It keeps sliding because there's always something more urgent. But at some point,
the lack of testing becomes the urgent thing. That's where we are now."

**Priya**: "You're both right. And I want to own my role in that pattern. But first,
let's talk about the systemic issues."

---

## 3. Systemic Issues Discussion

This section captured an extended discussion about the team's engineering practices,
culture, and process gaps. Priya explicitly asked everyone to speak freely.

**Priya**: "OK, I want to open this up. Before we get to action items, I think we
need to be really honest about where we are as an engineering team. I'm going to
start, and then I want to hear from everyone."

**Priya**: "I want to be honest here. This isn't Marcus's fault. I built this culture.
I'm the one who said 'just ship it' every time someone raised concerns about testing
or code review. Lina flagged staging. Danny asked about tests. I overruled all of it
because I prioritized speed."

**Priya**: "And I want to be specific. When Lina brought up staging two weeks ago, I
said 'we don't have time to set that up right now.' When Danny asked about tests, I
said 'let's get the feature out first.' When Marcus pushed billing.py in a single
commit, there was no review process to catch anything because I never set one up.
Every one of those was my call, and every one of them contributed to this incident."

**Marcus**: "I appreciate that, Priya, but I also need to own my part. I own the
code. I wrote billing.py in 3 days in a single file and didn't test the edge cases.
That's on me regardless of the culture. I could have written tests on my own initiative.
I could have read the Stripe docs more carefully. I could have asked Jess or Lina
to look at the code before I shipped it. I didn't do any of that."

**Jess**: "It's both. Marcus wrote the bug, but the system — our system — didn't
have any guardrails to catch it. No tests, no review, no staging. If any one of
those existed, this probably doesn't happen."

**Jess**: "Think about it. If we had code review, someone would have looked at the
idempotency logic and maybe caught the event ID vs. invoice ID issue. If we had
tests, a retry test case would have caught it. If we had staging, we would have
run the billing flow in a safe environment before pointing real customers at it.
Any single one of those guardrails would have been enough."

**Danny**: "I'm not going to say I told you so, but... I literally asked about
writing tests two weeks ago and was told to skip it."

**Danny**: "And I want to be clear — I'm not trying to score points here. I brought
it up because I was worried about exactly this kind of thing. Not specifically a
billing bug, but the general pattern of shipping code that handles important stuff
— money, customer data, authentication — without any safety net. It felt like a
matter of time."

**Priya**: "You're right. And I'm sorry. That was a mistake."

**Lina**: "So what are we actually going to change? Because I've been raising these
issues for a month and the answer has always been 'after the next milestone.' There's
always a next milestone."

**Lina**: "I don't want this to be another conversation where we all agree things
need to change and then nothing actually changes. I've seen that pattern before at
other companies. The post-mortem produces a list of action items, everyone feels
good about it, and then three weeks later we're back to the same habits because
the next deadline is looming."

**Priya**: "You're right. The pattern of deferring process has to stop. We can't
keep saying 'later.' We need to start now."

**Ravi**: "Can I jump in here? I want to give the business perspective."

**Priya**: "Please."

**Ravi**: "From the customer side — Nara was understanding but they're watching us
closely now. Belmont's legal team wants a formal response. Crestview was in a trial
period and they haven't decided whether to convert yet. This incident could cost
us the deal. We can't have another one of these."

**Ravi**: "I spoke with all three accounts this morning. Nara's CTO said, and I'm
quoting, 'mistakes happen, but we need to know this won't happen again.' They want
to see what we're doing to prevent recurrence. Belmont is a different story — their
legal team is involved and they want a formal written response. And Crestview...
honestly, I don't know where we stand with them. They were already on the fence
about the trial conversion, and this didn't help."

**Priya**: "That's helpful context, Ravi. Thank you. We need to factor the customer
communication into the action items."

**Priya**: "We won't. Here's what I'm proposing..."

## 4. Systemic Issues Identified

Priya summarized the systemic issues that emerged from the discussion. The team
reviewed and agreed on the risk levels by consensus.

| Issue | Description | Risk Level |
|-------|-------------|------------|
| No code review | All code ships without peer review | Critical |
| No automated tests | Zero test coverage across entire codebase | Critical |
| No staging environment | All deployments go directly to production | Critical |
| Monolithic files | Core logic in 2-3 massive files (routes.py: 2,247 lines, billing.py: 1,100+ lines) | High |
| No deployment process | Developers deploy from local machines at any time | High |
| No on-call rotation | No defined process for after-hours incidents | Medium |
| No documentation | Server config, cron jobs, and architecture undocumented | Medium |
| No database migration process | Schema changes applied directly to production DB | Medium |

**Lina**: "I want to add context on the monolithic files issue. routes.py is 2,247
lines in a single file. billing.py is over 1,100 lines. When everything is in one
file, there's no natural module boundary that forces you to think about interfaces
and contracts. The webhook handler, the charge creation logic, the refund logic,
the invoice generation — they're all tangled together in billing.py. That makes it
harder to test any one piece in isolation, and it makes it easier to introduce bugs
because a change to one function can have side effects on another."

**Marcus**: "Yeah, and it makes code review harder too, even once we have it. Reviewing
a diff in a 1,100-line file is way harder than reviewing changes to a focused module
with clear responsibilities."

**Jess**: "The deployment process one is also worth flagging. Right now, any of us
can deploy at any time from our local machine. There's no deploy queue, no deploy
log, no coordination. If two people deploy at the same time, we could end up in a
weird state. And there's no way to quickly roll back."

**Danny**: "We don't even have a consistent way to know what's currently running in
production. If someone asks me 'what version of billing.py is live right now,' I'd
have to SSH in and check."

**Priya**: "All noted. Let's get to the action items."

(next two weeks), and medium-term (next month). Each item was discussed, assigned
an owner, and given a deadline. The team agreed by consensus on priorities and
assignments.

## 6. Cultural Discussion

Priya transitioned the conversation to a broader discussion about team culture and
engineering values. She explicitly asked each person to share their perspective.

**Priya**: "Before we wrap up, I want to have a broader conversation. This incident
isn't just a technical failure — it's a cultural one. I want everyone to share what
they're thinking."

**Priya**: "I want to say something to the whole team. We built something real in
the last two weeks. 7 features, 2 enterprise customers, $60K ARR. That's incredible.
But we also shipped a bug that cost our customers $12K and damaged our reputation.
The lesson isn't that we were wrong to move fast — it's that we moved fast without
guardrails, and eventually that catches up to you."

**Priya**: "I've been thinking about this a lot since last night. I started this
company because I wanted to build something great, and I believed that speed was the
most important thing. Ship fast, iterate, figure it out. And that worked for the
first few months when it was just me and Jess and the stakes were lower. But we're
handling real money now. We have enterprise customers with legal teams. The stakes
are different, and our process needs to catch up."

**Marcus**: "I think the lesson is simpler. I wrote payments code in a single file
with no tests and no one looked at it. That's not fast, that's reckless. There's a
difference."

**Marcus**: "And I want to be accountable for that. I've been writing production code
for five years. I know better. I got caught up in the sprint energy and let my
standards slip. That's not something I want to repeat."

**Jess**: "Fast with guardrails is still fast. Code review doesn't have to take a
week. Tests don't have to cover everything. But zero review and zero tests is...
well, we found out what that gets you."

**Jess**: "I've worked at places with heavy process — two-week review cycles, ten
approvers, bureaucracy for every change. That's the other extreme and it kills
productivity. What we need is lightweight process. A 15-minute code review. A handful
of tests for the critical path. A staging deploy before production. None of that is
going to slow us down meaningfully, and it would have prevented this incident."

**Danny**: "I just want to say — I'm glad we're having this conversation. I've been
uncomfortable with the 'just ship it' thing for a while but didn't want to slow the
team down."

**Danny**: "I think part of the issue is that when you're the new person on the team
and the founder is saying 'ship it,' it's hard to push back. I raised the testing
question and got the 'later' answer, and I dropped it because I didn't want to be
the person who was blocking progress. But I should have pushed harder."

**Priya**: "That's on me, not you. If the culture makes it hard for people to raise
concerns, that's a leadership problem. I need to create an environment where
flagging risks is valued, not treated as friction."

**Lina**: "Same. I've been flagging risks and getting told 'later.' I hope 'later'
is now."

**Lina**: "I want to add — this is the kind of thing that accumulates. Each
individual shortcut feels small. Skip the review, skip the test, deploy straight to
production. But they compound. And by the time you notice, you're in a situation where
your entire billing system is a single untested file with no review history and no
staging environment. That's where we are."

**Priya**: "Later is now. Starting today, we review code. We write tests for critical
paths. We set up staging. We do this the right way. We can still be fast — we just
can't be reckless."

**Ravi**: "From the business side, I'd rather ship one feature a week with confidence
than three features a week with a ticking time bomb. The customer trust we lost is
harder to rebuild than any feature."

**Ravi**: "I want to share something from the Nara conversation this morning. Their
CTO said they chose Foldly because we were responsive and fast-moving. They liked
that we shipped features quickly. But he also said — and I'm paraphrasing — that
speed means nothing if the product isn't reliable. He said they can wait an extra
week for a feature, but they can't tolerate being overcharged. That really stuck
with me."

**Priya**: "That's a good way to frame it. Our customers chose us for speed, but
they'll leave us over reliability. We need to be both."

**Marcus**: "One more thing — I think we should be transparent with our customers
about the changes we're making. Not in a 'please don't leave us' way, but in a
'here's what we learned and here's what we're doing about it' way. Customers
respect that."

**Ravi**: "I agree. I'll factor that into the Belmont response and the follow-ups
with Nara and Crestview."

[2025-04-16 10:15:18] jess.tanaka
hey i started going through routes.py to check for similar issues. found something in the document upload handler

[2025-04-16 10:15:32] priya.kaur

[2025-04-16 10:15:45] jess.tanaka
the upload endpoint accepts any file type. there's no validation on file type, file size, or content type. someone could upload a 10GB binary and it'd go straight into our storage and crash the server

[2025-04-16 10:15:58] danny.reeves
:eyes:

[2025-04-16 10:16:12] lina.okafor
jess are you serious? there's no upload size limit?

[2025-04-16 10:16:24] jess.tanaka
completely serious. i went through the route handler line by line. `request.files['document']` is read directly, passed to `save_to_storage()`, no checks in between. the function doesn't even verify the file isn't empty

[2025-04-16 10:16:35] jess.tanaka
nope. the nginx proxy has a default 1MB limit but i checked and we overrode that to 100MB for the doc preview feature. so anything up to 100MB goes through unvalidated

[2025-04-16 10:16:47] marcus.webb
who overrode the nginx limit?

[2025-04-16 10:16:58] priya.kaur

[2025-04-16 10:17:10] jess.tanaka
it was in the nginx config commit from february. no blame — we needed larger uploads for the doc preview feature. the problem is we raised the limit at the proxy level without adding application-level validation

[2025-04-16 10:17:22] jess.tanaka
yeah i'll do it today. also going to add content-type verification so someone can't rename a .exe to .pdf and upload it

[2025-04-16 10:17:34] danny.reeves
how are you going to do the content-type verification? magic bytes?

[2025-04-16 10:17:45] lina.okafor

[2025-04-16 10:17:57] jess.tanaka
@danny.reeves yeah, python-magic. it reads the file header to determine the actual MIME type regardless of the file extension. so if someone renames malware.exe to report.pdf, python-magic will still identify it as application/x-executable

[2025-04-16 10:18:08] priya.kaur
good catch. add that too. let's do a systematic security review of all endpoints this week

[2025-04-16 10:18:19] lina.okafor
i can help with the rate limiting piece. we can use flask-limiter with a per-user-per-endpoint configuration. something like 10 uploads per minute per user should be plenty

[2025-04-16 10:18:43] lina.okafor

option A: duplicate the entire prod stack on a separate server. cost: ~$180/month. gives us a full mirror including DB, cron jobs, webhooks. downside: have to maintain two environments

option B: use docker-compose to spin up the entire stack locally. cost: just developer time. downside: doesn't test against real infrastructure (load balancer, DNS, etc.)

option C: feature flags. deploy to prod but gate new features behind flags. cost: low. downside: doesn't test the deployment itself, just the code path

[2025-04-16 14:05:33] jess.tanaka
marcus — quick question on the transaction rollback. if the stripe cancellation succeeds and then the local delete fails and we try to re-subscribe on stripe, are you using the same subscription ID or creating a new one?

[2025-04-16 14:05:47] marcus.webb
same subscription ID. stripe supports reactivating a canceled subscription within a certain window. i'm calling `stripe.Subscription.modify()` with `cancel_at_period_end=False` to undo the cancellation. so the customer's subscription continues as if nothing happened

[2025-04-16 14:06:01] jess.tanaka
ok good. and what if the re-subscription fails?

[2025-04-16 14:06:14] marcus.webb
then we log the error with full context — customer ID, subscription ID, stripe error — and raise an exception that will trigger our error handler. at that point it's a manual intervention situation. we'd need to either re-cancel on stripe or re-delete locally depending on the desired state

[2025-04-16 14:06:27] jess.tanaka
makes sense. just wanted to make sure the failure path was covered. continuing the review

[2025-04-16 14:12:44] jess.tanaka
question on the seat count query — you're using a LEFT JOIN to bring in the users table. is there an index on `users.deactivated_at`? if we get a lot of users, this query could slow down

[2025-04-16 14:12:58] marcus.webb
there's an index on `org_id` and `last_login` but not on `deactivated_at`. should i add one?

[2025-04-16 14:13:11] jess.tanaka
not right now. with our current data volume it won't matter. but flag it as a TODO for when we scale. add a comment in the query

[2025-04-16 14:13:24] marcus.webb
done

[2025-04-16 14:22:33] jess.tanaka
reviewed. couple comments:

1. the transaction rollback is good but you're catching a bare `Exception`. can you narrow that to `stripe.error.StripeError` and `sqlalchemy.exc.SQLAlchemyError`?

2. the deactivated user filter queries the users table for each customer separately. if we have 100 customers this becomes 100 queries. can you do a single join query?

3. the pydantic models look great. one thing — the `StripeWebhookEvent` model should validate that `type` is one of our handled event types, not just a string

[2025-04-16 14:22:47] danny.reeves
agreed on #3. use a Literal type or an Enum for the event types. that way pydantic rejects events we don't handle right at the parsing layer

[2025-04-16 14:23:01] marcus.webb
good catches. fixing all three

[2025-04-16 14:23:13] lina.okafor
+1 on the exception narrowing. bare `except Exception` is how you swallow errors you didn't expect and end up debugging phantom issues at 2am

[2025-04-16 14:23:25] marcus.webb
yeah i know, i was being lazy. fixing it now

[2025-04-16 14:35:07] marcus.webb
@jess.tanaka question on comment #2 — the single join query. i'm doing this:
```sql
SELECT org_id, COUNT(*) AS active_seats
FROM users
WHERE last_login > ? AND deactivated_at IS NULL
GROUP BY org_id
```
so it counts all active non-deactivated users across all orgs in a single query. that work?

[2025-04-16 14:35:21] jess.tanaka
perfect. much better than N+1 queries. also add a WHERE clause to only include orgs that have active subscriptions — no point counting seats for free-tier or churned customers

[2025-04-16 14:35:34] marcus.webb
good point. adding a JOIN on subscriptions where status = 'active'

[2025-04-16 14:45:18] marcus.webb
updated the PR. addressed all three comments. also added a `HANDLED_EVENT_TYPES` constant so we can see at a glance which webhook events we process

[2025-04-19 14:30:22] priya.kaur
i want to document our new deployment process before we start using staging on monday. writing it up now. here's the draft:

---
**Foldly Deployment Process v1.0**

**Effective: 2025-04-21**

1. Create a feature branch from `main`
2. Make changes, commit to branch
3. Open a Pull Request against `main`
4. Automated tests run on PR (CI pipeline)
   - All tests must pass before merge is allowed
5. At least 1 teammate must review and approve the PR
   - For billing/auth/data changes: 2 approvals required
6. Merge PR to `main`
7. Merge triggers automatic deployment to staging
8. Engineer verifies the change on staging
   - For billing changes: test with Stripe test mode
   - For auth changes: test SSO flow
   - For document changes: test upload/download/search
9. Engineer promotes from staging to production
   - Promotion requires passing staging verification
10. Monitor production for 30 minutes post-deploy
    - Check Sentry for new errors
    - Check Grafana for anomalies (when available)

**Emergency Hotfix Process**:
- Same flow, but reviewer has 15-minute SLA
- Staging verification can be abbreviated
- Post-deploy monitoring extended to 1 hour
- Incident channel stays open until monitoring period ends

**Never**:
- Deploy from a local machine directly to production
- Merge without tests passing
- Deploy without at least 1 code review
---

================================================================================

Author        : Lina Okafor
Reviewers     : Priya Kaur, Danny Reeves
Status        : Approved

Supersedes    : N/A (no prior pipeline documentation existed)
